title: Jamaican beef patty
subtitle: Flaky turmeric pastry with spiced beef filling
excerpt: A spiced minced beef filling of scotch bonnet, allspice, spring onion, and thyme, encased in a short, flaky pastry stained yellow with turmeric. The pastry is the signature element: richer than shortcrust, with a pronounced flaky pull when you bite through it. Baked, not fried. The filling should be well-seasoned and moist without being wet.
sourceNotes: The Jamaican beef patty evolved from the Cornish pasty brought by British colonisers, transformed through the addition of West African and Indian spices into a distinctly Jamaican product. The turmeric pastry and allspice-scotch bonnet filling are documented in Jamaican cooking literature from the 20th century. In the UK the patty is associated with the Jamaican community and appears in Caribbean bakeries across London, Birmingham, and Manchester.

content.text: The pastry uses lard for flakiness and turmeric for colour. Keep the fat cold and handle the dough minimally, overworked pastry is tough, not flaky. The dough should look rough and streaky after mixing; it comes together in the fridge rest.

The filling must be completely cool before filling the patties. Hot filling melts the fat in the pastry and the dough becomes greasy and difficult to seal. Make the filling first and chill it while you make the pastry.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}}, {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{thyme}}, {{allspice}}, {{curry-powder}}, {{black-pepper}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{beef-mince}} and cook, breaking it up, for 8 minutes until browned and dry. Add the {{spring-onion}} and {{breadcrumbs-dried}} and stir to combine. The mixture should be dry enough to hold its shape when scooped. Taste and adjust seasoning. Cool completely.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{turmeric}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Add the cold {{lard}} and rub it into the flour with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with some pea-sized lumps remaining. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time (about 5 to 6 tablespoons) until the dough just comes together. Do not over-mix. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Roll the pastry to 3 mm on a floured surface and cut into circles about 18 cm across (a small plate works as a template). Place a generous tablespoon of cold filling on one half of each circle, leaving a 1 cm border. Fold the pastry over and press the edges firmly. Crimp with a fork. Place on a lined baking tray. Brush lightly with water or beaten egg. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the pastry is cooked through and has deepened to a golden-orange colour.

items.fix: Add another tablespoon of water before re-chilling. Bring the dough to cool room temperature before rolling
items.cause: Dough too dry or too cold

items.fix: Press the crimped edge firmly before baking. The breadcrumbs in the filling absorb excess moisture, ensure the filling is properly dry before using
items.cause: Edges not sealed firmly, or filling too wet

items.fix: Ensure the oven is fully preheated. The turmeric gives a yellow baseline; a proper golden-orange comes from oven heat, not the spice

content.text: The Jamaican beef patty is the most widely sold Jamaican food product in the world. In Jamaica it is sold from patty shops, bakeries, and bus station vendors, a cheap, portable meal. In Britain it is the everyday food of the Caribbean community, sold in Caribbean bakeries and supermarkets across the country. The Tastee patty chain from Jamaica has been making patties since 1966; the Juici Patties chain since 1983. The patty is as Jamaican as oxtail stew but far more accessible.
